items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
Vague doubts haunt me once again...
Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: Perfect match to the approved rendering. It preserves the mock-heroic, high-brow phrasing (“Vague doubts haunt me…”) that creates the comic contrast with the petty theft logic. It also correctly avoids turning «у Шпака/у посла» into simple possession (“Shpak has…”) and instead frames it as the parallel ‘case’/association, which is the intended joke per reference analysis.

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: Perfect match to the approved rendering. The translation keeps the repetitive, banal Soviet-style politeness and the absurd self-introduction (“I’m the Tsar… The Tsar…”) that drives the humor (era/register clash + drunken stiffness).

items.translation: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Perfect match to the approved rendering. The imperative ‘I demand’ preserves the sudden authoritarian, power-drunk persona shift that is central to the gag.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: Perfect match to the approved rendering. It keeps the elevated, archaic-sounding melancholy (“I am in sorrow”) and the socially jarring “old woman,” which is the comedic friction in the modern setting.

items.translation: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: Perfect match to the approved rendering. It correctly makes “demons” the agent (subject) and preserves the paranoid-mystical misreading of mundane technology that the joke relies on.

items.translation: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Perfect match to the approved rendering. The elevated biblical diction (“Behold… life-giving cross”) preserves the Tsar’s religious certainty and the comic contrast with a mechanical/elevator explanation.

items.translation: The floor is open to everyone!
items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: Perfect match to the approved rendering. It conveys the MC-like command that triggers collective dancing, keeping the performative, ‘announcement’ feel rather than a flat literal “Everyone dance!”

items.translation: Now, this was a good call!
items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: Perfect match to the approved rendering. It keeps the breezy, self-satisfied criminal cheerfulness and the idiomatic punch of the line in English.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: Perfect match to the approved rendering. Crucially, it avoids calling the eggplant item “caviar” in English, preserving the joke’s inversion without confusing viewers unfamiliar with the Russian ‘икра’ food category.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: Perfect match to the approved rendering. It preserves the comic rhythm of the escalating inventory and the slightly incoherent self-correction/afterthought quality that signals exaggeration.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: Perfect match to the approved rendering. The calm, sing-song repetition is preserved, maintaining the darkly comic resignation about psychiatric ‘treatment’ that the scene plays on.

summary.interpretation: 0.00 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.00 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
